name: Warli Painting
history: Warli art originates from the Warli tribe of Maharashtra. Using only white pigment on mud-coated walls, these geometric paintings depict daily life, harvests, and weddings. The circle, triangle, and square form the universe of Warli — the circle represents the sun and moon, the triangle mountains and trees, and the square a sacred enclosure.

name: Madhubani Painting
history: Madhubani or Mithila painting originated in Bihar and has been practiced for over three millennia. Women traditionally painted these on the walls of their homes during festivals and weddings. The art uses vibrant colors derived from natural sources — turmeric, indigo, and flowers. Fish, lotus, and the sun are sacred motifs representing fertility and prosperity.

name: Phulkari Embroidery
history: Phulkari means 'flower work' in Punjabi. This embroidery tradition has been passed down through generations of Punjabi women who embroider shawls and dupattas with silk thread on coarse cotton cloth. A Phulkari was lovingly made by mothers for their daughters' wedding trousseau — a gift of love and identity. Today fewer than 140 women practice this ancient art.

name: Gond Art
history: Gond art comes from the Gond tribe of central India. These paintings depict nature — animals, trees, and gods — using intricate patterns of dots and dashes. The Gondi believe that seeing a good image brings good luck, so the art is both spiritual and celebratory. Every line in a Gond painting is filled with smaller patterns creating a breathing, living artwork.

name: Toda Embroidery
history: Toda embroidery is the rarest textile tradition in India, practiced exclusively by the Toda tribe of the Nilgiri Hills. Only 43 active artists remain. The geometric red and black patterns are embroidered on white buffalo milk cloth and were traditionally made only for sacred purposes. Each piece takes months to complete and carries the spiritual identity of the entire Toda community.

name: Pattachitra
history: Pattachitra is a cloth-based scroll painting from Odisha, rooted in the Jagannath cult of Puri. The word means 'picture on cloth'. Artists use natural colors made from stone, conch shells, and plant dyes. The distinctive feature is the elaborate floral border that frames every composition. This art form has been the hereditary occupation of the Chitrakar community for generations.

name: Block Printing
history: Rajasthani block printing is one of India's most celebrated textile traditions. Carved wooden blocks are dipped in natural dyes and stamped onto fabric in rhythmic patterns. Sanganer is famous for white backgrounds and floral prints, while Bagru uses earthy tones and bold geometric patterns. The craft has been practiced by the Chippa community for over 800 years and remains central to Rajasthan's cultural identity.

name: Chikankari
history: Chikankari is the delicate white-on-white embroidery of Lucknow, said to have been introduced by Nur Jahan, wife of Emperor Jahangir. The word 'chikan' means 'fine embroidery' in Persian. Using shadow work and intricate floral motifs, artisans embroider on muslin so fine it was called 'woven air'. Today women in Lucknow's old city neighborhoods keep this 400-year tradition alive.
